turns.content: I'm trying to debug a failing CI pipeline that fetches S3 logs, and I think the issue is how I'm handling pagination. Can you explain why my current code might be broken?

The most common mistake beginners make with aws-sdk-rust is trying to use a standard loop over iterators without checking if the paginator has finished. If your code simply iterates until exhaustion, it will hang forever because the GetObjectsRequest never returns all objects in one call. You need to explicitly check whether the response contains a NextToken field before making another request.
That makes sense. But I'm confused about how exactly the pagination helper works inside the client builder. Should I be manually calling that method or is it automatic?

The pagination helpers are part of the AWS SDK's built-in paginator trait, which you get when you instantiate the service client via the builder pattern. When you call methods like GetObjectsRequest on a properly configured client, they automatically handle token-based pagination under the hood using the aws-sdk crate internals. You don't manually invoke any extra method to start it.

Okay so if I use the builder pattern correctly, I just call the request method and it handles everything? But my pipeline still fails with a timeout error even after fixing that loop. What else could be wrong?

The next common pitfall is ignoring credential configuration in your environment setup. If you're not setting the AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY variables before your tool starts, the aws-sdk-rust client will fail to authenticate and eventually hit a timeout while retrying. You must ensure credentials are available before any HTTP request is made.
Ah! I was relying on default credential chains but my CI environment doesn't have those set up. That's probably the root cause. How do I configure this properly for a script running in a container?

For scripts running in containers, you should pass credentials explicitly via the builder pattern using the CredentialsProvider trait instead of relying on environment variables. You can construct a static credential provider like DefaultCredentialsProvider or use a SharedCredentialsProvider if needed, ensuring your aws-sdk client always has valid tokens regardless of external state.

I came back to coding five years ago and I'm trying to set up the official AWS SDK in Rust, but why does this even work this way? I thought setting up credentials would just be a simple config file like other languages.

You aren't alone with that frustration. In the Rust ecosystem, we rely on traits and modules instead of global state files for security reasons. The aws-sdk crate requires you to explicitly create a client using specific constructors because it needs to know which AWS service region you are targeting before any operation happens.
Okay so I need to load my credentials first, right? Can't I just pass them into the constructor?

That is exactly where your assumption goes wrong. You cannot simply pass credentials into a client; instead, the aws-sdk crate requires you to retrieve your credentials from an environment variable or use the CredentialProvider trait to load them dynamically.
So I need to manually manage the credential loading? That sounds like a lot of boilerplate code for just this one step.

It does feel like more work upfront, but that design choice ensures your application stays secure and doesn't accidentally leak sensitive data into environment variables or config files. The SDK uses a builder pattern where you chain methods together to construct the client object before sending any requests.
